full_text:
NMMC ramps up measures amid heatwave
Navi Mumbai: As the city grapples with a prolonged spell of intense heat, civic authorities are stepping up preparedness to address rising health concerns. Over the past month, temperatures have consistently ranged between 34°C and 40°C, with humidity levels between 60% and 76%, making conditions feel even more oppressive. Compared to last year’s relatively moderate April temperatures of 36–37°C, this season has witnessed a noticeable spike. Warm nights, averaging 25–28°C, have offered little respite, adding to public discomfort.
The Navi Mumbai Municipal Corporation has intensified awareness campaigns across civic hospitals and government institutions. Through informational videos and guidance sessions, residents are being educated about hydration, preventive measures, and early signs of heat-related illnesses.
“There is a strong focus not just on treatment but on prevention,” said Dr. Prashant Jawade. “We are actively spreading awareness on what people should and should not do during extreme heat, and how to respond if someone shows symptoms of heatstroke. Early signs like nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and dry skin must not be ignored.”
He further added, “Healthcare facilities under NMMC are equipped to manage emergencies, with adequate stocks of Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS), sufficient beds, and dedicated care units. A special 10-bed heatstroke ward has also been set up to ensure prompt treatment for critical cases.”
Residents, especially those working outdoors, say the situation is becoming increasingly challenging. “It’s really difficult to even go out during peak hours,” said Jees George. “Because of my work, I have to stay outside for hours. Even if I carry water and wear a cap, after some time it’s of no use. The rising temperature is a serious concern, especially for outdoor workers and children. I’ve lived here for two decades, and in the past few years, the heat has become unbearable. Rapid urbanisation and construction could be contributing to this.”
With forecasts indicating continued hot and humid conditions, authorities are urging citizens to stay hydrated, avoid direct sun exposure during peak hours, and remain cautious during the ongoing heatwave.

title: Heatwave Protection: Kids Face Heatstroke Horror! Ayurveda Expert’s Tips For Survival - DNP India
full_text:
Heatwave Protection: As temperatures soar each summer, heatwaves are no longer just uncomfortable but dangerous too, especially for young children. This is simply because young bodies of children are less efficient compared to those of adults to regulate temperature, which makes them more vulnerable to dehydration, exhaustion, and in severe cases, heatstroke. Thankfully, you can always count on a Ayurveda that offers a time-tested, holistic approach to protecting children during extreme heat.
Drawing from centuries of wisdom, Ayurvedic practices focus on balancing the body’s internal heat, strengthening resilience and nourishing from within. With the right foods, herbs and daily habits, parents can create a powerful shield against the harsh effects of rising temperatures.
Heatwave Protection-Why Children Are at Greater Risk?
Naturally, children have higher metabolic activity and tend to spend more time outdoors, often unaware of early warning signs like dizziness or fatigue. Heatstroke can escalate quickly, presenting symptoms such as flushed skin, rapid pulse, nausea and confusion. Prevention, therefore, becomes far more important than treatment.
The Power of Dates
In summer diets, dates are often overlooked because of their natural sweetness, but when consumed in moderation and soaked, they can be incredibly beneficial. Rich in natural sugars, iron and potassium, soaked dates provide sustained energy without overheating the body. They help replenish minerals lost through sweating and support overall stamina.
Munakka: Nature’s Rehydration Aid
Munakka, or dried black raisins, is a staple in Ayurvedic remedies for heat exhaustion. When soaked overnight, munakka becomes cooling and hydrating. It supports digestion, relieves constipation (a common summer issue) and helps maintain fluid balance.
Giving children a few soaked munakkas in the morning can help prevent dryness and internal heat buildup. It also supports liver function, which plays a role in detoxifying the body during stressful conditions like extreme heat.
Mulethi: The Soothing Root
Mulethi, or licorice root, is a powerful herb known for its cooling and soothing properties. It helps reduce inflammation, supports respiratory health and combats dryness caused by hot winds.
In summer, mulethi-infused water or a mild herbal drink can help children stay hydrated while calming the body’s internal heat. It is especially useful for kids prone to throat irritation or dry cough due to hot, dusty environments.
Amla: The Ultimate Summer Superfruit
Amla, or Indian gooseberry, is one of Ayurveda’s most revered fruits. Rich in vitamin C and antioxidants, it strengthens immunity, improves digestion and most importantly, cools the body from within.
Amla juice diluted with water, or fresh amla chutneys, can be incorporated into daily meals. It helps combat fatigue, supports skin health (often affected by sun exposure) and enhances overall vitality.
For children, amla candy or murabba (in controlled portions) can be an appealing way to include this powerful ingredient in their diet.
Natural Coolants from the Kitchen
Ayurveda emphasizes using simple kitchen ingredients to balance the body’s heat.
Kokum: The Coastal Cooler
Kokum, widely used in western India, is known for its refreshing and cooling properties. It aids digestion, prevents acidity and reduces body heat. Kokum sherbet is an excellent alternative to packaged drinks and can be given to children during peak summer afternoons.
Imli (Tamarind): Tangy Relief
Imli, or tamarind, has a natural cooling effect and helps combat dehydration. Rich in electrolytes, it supports digestion and relieves heat-induced sluggishness. Tamarind water or light chutneys can be a great addition to meals, stimulating appetite in children who tend to eat less in the heat.
Anardana: Digestive Support
Dried pomegranate seeds, or anardana, are both cooling and digestive. They help maintain gut health, which often gets compromised during summer due to irregular eating patterns and dehydration. A pinch of anardana powder in meals or drinks can enhance flavour while supporting internal balance.
Mishri: Sweet Cooling Energy
Mishri, or rock sugar, is considered cooling in Ayurveda and is often used in summer beverages. Unlike refined sugar, mishri provides a gentle sweetness while helping to reduce body heat. It can be combined with fennel seeds or added to drinks like lemon water or milk to create a refreshing, child-friendly beverage.
